pvals = []
models = {}

for cat in ["A", "B", "C", "D"]:
    df[f"is_{cat}"] = (df["outcome"] == cat).astype(int)
    
    full      = smf.logit(f"is_{cat} ~ C(llm) + C(prompt) + C(llm):C(prompt)", data=df).fit(disp=0)
    no_inter  = smf.logit(f"is_{cat} ~ C(llm) + C(prompt)", data=df).fit(disp=0)
    no_llm    = smf.logit(f"is_{cat} ~ C(prompt)", data=df).fit(disp=0)
    no_prompt = smf.logit(f"is_{cat} ~ C(llm)", data=df).fit(disp=0)

    models[cat] = {
        "p_llm":    lrt(no_inter, no_llm,    df_diff=1),
        "p_prompt": lrt(no_inter, no_prompt, df_diff=2),
        "p_inter":  lrt(full,     no_inter,  df_diff=2),
    }

# Collect all p-values and correct jointly
all_pvals = [(cat, effect, models[cat][effect]) 
             for cat in models for effect in models[cat]]

_, corrected, _, _ = multipletests([p for _,_,p in all_pvals], method="fdr_bh")

for (cat, effect, raw), corr in zip(all_pvals, corrected):
    print(f"{cat} | {effect:12s} raw={raw:.4f}  corrected={corr:.4f}")
This corrects across all tests jointly (4 categories × 3 effects = 12 tests), which is the conservative and correct thing to do.

Summary
Your situation
Samples per cell	~18-19 ✅ workable
Prompt treatment	Fixed effect (3 is too few for random)
Binary outcome	Standard logistic regression + LRT
3-4 category outcome	One binary model per category
Correction	Benjamini-Hochberg across all tests
